statusLine: European fuel security is under pressure as 19 of 27 EU countries now face critical shortages in at least one fuel type, with oil prices surging 51% since late February due to Middle East supply disruptions.
fullAnalysis:
European fuel reserves are under significant strain following recent strategic reserve releases in response to Middle East supply disruptions. Currently, 19 of 27 EU countries face critical shortages (below 90 days) in at least one fuel category. The situation is most severe in Eastern Europe, where Romania shows critical levels across all three fuel types with only 33.2 days of petrol reserves, while Poland faces similar challenges with particularly acute jet fuel shortages at just 21.3 days.

Jet fuel presents the most widespread concern, with 17 of 27 countries below the 90-day threshold. Portugal's jet fuel reserves stand at just 23.1 days, while several major economies including Germany (61.4 days), France (59.6 days), and the Netherlands (59.0 days) also show critical jet fuel levels. Diesel supplies are similarly tight, with 15 countries below 90 days, though some nations like Finland (187.4 days) and the Netherlands (215.7 days) maintain substantial diesel buffers.

Brent crude oil prices have surged to $109.45 per barrel, marking a dramatic 51% increase since the late-February escalation began. This price spike is directly reflected in retail fuel costs, with significant variations across the bloc - Dutch consumers now pay €2.32 per liter for petrol while Maltese drivers pay just €1.34. The combination of tight physical supplies and elevated prices creates compounding pressure on European energy security.
keyPoints: 19 of 27 EU countries face critical fuel shortages in at least one category, with Romania and Poland showing the most severe multi-fuel deficits
Jet fuel reserves are most vulnerable EU-wide, with 17 countries below the 90-day safety threshold including major economies
Oil prices have surged 51% since late February to $109.45/barrel, driving significant fuel price disparities across member states
Eastern European nations face the most acute shortages, while Nordic and some Western countries maintain stronger reserve positions
Strategic reserve releases following Middle East disruptions have left many countries with insufficient buffer stocks
